DJ Sell Caterpillar Stock, Analyst Says, Because This Time Is Different -- Barrons.com


By Al Root

Investors typically look to buy economically sensitive, industrial shares when times are the worst because those stocks usually race higher from the depths of a downturn as things start to look better. This time may be different, largely because of prospects for energy prices.

Bank of America is taking a downbeat view on Caterpillar stock (ticker: CAT). The bank doesn't expect the outlook for the maker of heavy machinery to improve even after the Covid-19 pandemic passes and the global economy goes back to work.

Bank of America analyst Ross Gilardi cut his rating on Caterpillar shares to the equivalent of Sell from Hold on Monday. His target price is $115 a share, very close to current levels.

"The traditional playbook has been to buy CAT in the depths of recession as central banks inject massive monetary and fiscal stimulus to guide a global recovery," wrote Gilardi in a Monday research report. "We don't subscribe to this theory this time."

Benchmark crude-oil prices are down more than 60% year to date, even after OPEC and Russia agreed to cut output over the weekend. Lower oil prices means less cash flow for oil producers. That, in turn, will crimp their capital-spending budgets and limit demand for Cat products.

Caterpillar generates about one-third of its sales from its Energy and Transportation segment, which includes business from energy customers. The division supports oil and gas companies, as well as marine transport and railroads. Products include gas turbines and generators, as well as reciprocating engines.

"Cat derives only 10% of revenue directly from oil and gas," said Gilardi. "But its Construction and Mining products touch the energy sector in many places too."

Cat stock does have its positives. The shares yield about 3.6%, and Gilardi noted that the company's balance sheet remains strong. But there are higher yields to be had in today's marketplace. Energy Transfer (ET) for instance, a customer of Caterpillar's Energy and Transportation business, yields about 20.4%.

Energy Transfer is, essentially, a master limited partnership, a corporate structure common in the energy world that avoids double taxation of corporate profits and dividend paid to investors. MLP assets generally have higher yields than common stocks, but the differential between those for Caterpillar and Energy Transfer has widened substantially in recent weeks.

A dividend yield of 3.6% alone isn't enough for Bank of America to recommend Cat stock.

Caterpillar shares are down 7.1% Monday, following the downgrade. For the year to date, Cat stock is off about 21%, worse than the drops in the Dow Jones Industrial Average and S&P 500 over the same span.

The stock is trading for about 14 times estimated 2021 earnings, similar to other industrial companies, but a discount to the broader market's valuation ratio of 16 times.

Gilardi isn't alone in his concern. Slightly fewer than 50% of the Wall Street analysts covering the company rate the shares at Buy. The average Buy-rating ratio for stocks in the Dow is 55%.

Next up for Caterpillar investors will be first-quarter earnings, reported late this month. Like many companies, the manufacturer withdrew its financial forecasts for the full year in March. Before the Covid-19 pandemic, management expected to earn about $9.25 a share in 2020.

Investors will look for an update on the outlook for profits, as well as how government stimulus, including infrastructure spending, will affect its business in 2020 and beyond.

TLDR : CAT sell equipment to energy sector. 另外, FYI, 真係即刻開基建既話contractor mobilize equipment 要6個月時間+最快3-6個月design.
